84.Option: The lower seroconversion rates in South America may be due to the use of different diagnostic tests, which could introduce measurement bias in serological assessment
Evidence: In the Central American programs in Yoro, Honduras and Olopa, Guatemala, 50.2% and 50.8% of patients, respectively, had adverse events related to treatment (Table 3). In Yoro, most of the adverse events were mild, with no moderate cases and 3 severe cases due to neurological adverse events (neuromuscular disturbances of the lower limbs after 6 weeks of treatment). The most frequent adverse events were gastrointestinal disorders (26.8%, mainly epigastralgia and/or abdominal pain, and less frequently nausea and/or vomiting and anorexia), followed by dermatological conditions (13.0%, mainly pruritus and less frequently maculopapular exanthema) and neurological problems (10.4%, mainly neuromuscular disturbances). In Olopa, 80.9% (51/63) of the adverse events were mild, 14.3% (9/63) moderate, and 4.8% (3/63) severe (2 neuromuscular and 1 cutaneous). Adverse events were 26% dermatological in nature, 25% gastrointestinal, 23% neuromuscular, and 26% other types. In both Yoro and Olopa, no differences were seen in the proportion of adverse events depending on age or sex (Chi-square test).

Lower rates of treatment-related adverse events were observed in the Bolivian programs. In Entre Ríos, adverse events were observed in 25.6% of treated children, with increasing risk in older age groups (12% in <5 years old; 25% 10–14). In Sucre, 37.9% of patients had adverse events, also with increasing risk in older groups (13.4% in <5 years old; 50% 15–18). In Entre Ríos, 56% of adverse events were dermatological, 25% digestive, and 18% neuromuscular, of which 11% were mixed. In Sucre, 68.5% of adverse events were dermatological. In both programs, the majority of side effects were mild, with risk increasing with age.

The fundamental program component of screening and diagnosis used differing diagnostic protocols adapted to the contexts of each country/region. For diagnosis in our programs, the two tests selected were the two with minimum acceptable sensitivity and specificity (ideally 99–100%) and which could be feasibly implemented at the primary health care level [6],[16]. Filter paper blood samples were used in the earlier programs mainly for sensitivity and adapted ease of use (ie, no need for centrifugation, relatively easy to supply/refill, portability) in remote rural settings. We introduced the use of Chagas Stat-Pak RDT in the Bolivian programs and carried out a field evaluation using whole blood samples. Recent studies using this RDT have shown relatively low sensitivity (93–94%) compared with conventional tests [9],[17],[18], and this limited sensitivity must be considered in the use of this test. A whole-blood RDT with high sensitivity would be ideal for screening and diagnosis in resource-limited settings [19].
84.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that different diagnostic protocols were used in different regions, with the Bolivian programs using Chagas Stat-Pak RDT which has lower sensitivity compared to conventional tests. This difference in diagnostic methods could introduce measurement bias in serological assessments, which is a critical factor to consider when interpreting the observed differences in seroconversion rates between Central and South America.

84.Option: The timing of seroconversion may be delayed in South American populations, suggesting that current follow-up periods may be insufficient to detect true treatment efficacy
Evidence: Seroconversion rates observed in Entre Ríos and Sucre in Bolivia were much lower. Preliminary results of overall seroconversion post-treatment was 5.4% (59/1,101) in Entre Ríos by up to 60 months post-treatment, with over 950 of the patients sampled having had follow-up later than 18 months post-treatment. Seroconversion rates were found to be lower in older age groups compared with younger ones in Entre Ríos: 24.2% (16/66) <5 years old; 4.6% (14/303) 5–9 years old; 1.9% (12/638) 10–14 years old, at 18–60 months follow-up. To date, of 276 patients followed up between 9 and 27 months post-treatment, no patient has been found to have seroconverted in Sucre.

The seroconversion rates detected in treated patients were relatively high in the Central American projects, Yoro, Honduras and Olopa, Guatemala, showing that therapy can clear T. cruzi infection. However, seroconversion was far lower in the South American Bolivian projects in Entre Ríos and Sucre. The findings in Bolivia are similar to those reported from earlier studies in Argentina and Brazil [22]–[25]. Also, seroconversion was detected earlier in the Central American programs compared with the Bolivian programs. Previous research has shown that in South America seroconversion is sometimes not detected until 5–7 years later [26]. Thus, the higher and earlier seroconversion we detected in Central America supports previously reported findings [27] and may have important public health implications.
84.Rationale: The passage notes that seroconversion in South American programs was detected later, sometimes not until 5-7 years post-treatment, compared to earlier detection in Central America. This suggests that the current follow-up periods in South American programs may be insufficient to detect true treatment efficacy, making this a critical factor to consider when interpreting the observed differences in seroconversion rates.

84.Option: The lower seroconversion rates in South America may be due to higher rates of reinfection in endemic areas, which should be evaluated through repeated serological testing over time
Evidence: The seroconversion rates detected in treated patients were relatively high in the Central American projects, Yoro, Honduras and Olopa, Guatemala, showing that therapy can clear T. cruzi infection. However, seroconversion was far lower in the South American Bolivian projects in Entre Ríos and Sucre. The findings in Bolivia are similar to those reported from earlier studies in Argentina and Brazil [22]–[25]. Also, seroconversion was detected earlier in the Central American programs compared with the Bolivian programs. Previous research has shown that in South America seroconversion is sometimes not detected until 5–7 years later [26]. Thus, the higher and earlier seroconversion we detected in Central America supports previously reported findings [27] and may have important public health implications.

The differences in seroconversion rates may be explained by a number of reasons. One primary explanation may be based on the presence of different parasite lineages in different geographic regions, with T. cruzi type I predominating in Central America and T. cruzi type II in South America, with varying degrees of overlap [28]. Because of the potential differences in T. cruzi subtypes present in Honduras and Guatemala compared with Bolivia, drug treatment effectiveness may have differed. Another factor to consider is the time between vector control activity and drug treatment, since cases (mostly asymptomatic) closer to the acute phase of the disease can possibly account for more rapid seroconversion. Also, statistical limitations of our data analysis may exist due to the varying age groups and varying times of post-treatment follow-up in the four projects, as has been examined in other studies [29]. Finally, differences in immune response among populations may play a role.
84.Rationale: The passage does not mention reinfection as a factor affecting seroconversion rates. Instead, it discusses factors like parasite lineage differences, time between vector control and treatment, and immune response variations. While reinfection is a plausible hypothesis, it is not supported by the evidence provided in the passage, making this option incorrect.

84.Option: The higher seroconversion rates in Central America may reflect greater treatment adherence, which should be assessed through direct monitoring of medication intake in future studies
Evidence: For treatment and compliance, a large number and proportion of patients started and finished treatment according to protocol in our programs, with over 90% of patients completing >55 days of treatment. Access to treatment, follow-up, and referral of complicated cases were successful elements of the protocol. Relatively low dropout before treatment and low default rates (mostly migrations of patients and adverse reactions) were observed. However, in one program, Sucre, about 9% of diagnosed patients did not start treatment. The main reasons for this were migration, reluctance to start treatment (after counseling and informed consent), pregnant or lactating mothers, and treatment being offered by MOH national programs. Still, overall we found that Chagas treatment and follow-up can be achieved with adequately trained, sensitized, and motivated health staff and family members in both rural remote and periurban settings. The family-based approach for daily drug administration and compliance was key for Chagas disease because of the length of therapy and occurrence of adverse events.

The observed differences in seroconversion rates may be explained by a number of reasons. One primary explanation may be based on the presence of different parasite lineages in different geographic regions, with T. cruzi type I predominating in Central America and T. cruzi type II in South America, with varying degrees of overlap [28]. Because of the potential differences in T. cruzi subtypes present in Honduras and Guatemala compared with Bolivia, drug treatment effectiveness may have differed. Another factor to consider is the time between vector control activity and drug treatment, since cases (mostly asymptomatic) closer to the acute phase of the disease can possibly account for more rapid seroconversion. Also, statistical limitations of our data analysis may exist due to the varying age groups and varying times of post-treatment follow-up in the four projects, as has been examined in other studies [29]. Finally, differences in immune response among populations may play a role.
84.Rationale: The passage indicates that treatment adherence was high in both Central and South American programs, with over 90% of patients completing the majority of their treatment. It does not provide evidence to suggest that treatment adherence was significantly higher in Central America, making this option incorrect.

Evidence: The only sand fly with significant niche identity with Leishmania spp. was Lu. cruciata (Figure 8 and Table 5).

86.Option: The detection of B. pseudomallei-specific DNA in aerosols at Site G, which is located 5 km downwind from the source field, indicates that aerosols can travel the distance required for transmission during the typhoon season

Winds that blow across the surface of porous media can carry tiny droplets or solid particles (<50 mm) for several thousands of kilometers.
86.Rationale: While this option correctly identifies the 5 km distance and the presence of B. pseudomallei DNA in aerosols at Site G, it only establishes the physical possibility of transport without directly linking it to the specific transmission pathway. The option lacks the critical genetic evidence that would confirm the aerosolized DNA actually originated from the source field and could cause disease in humans. The study provides more compelling evidence through molecular typing that directly links the environmental and clinical isolates.

86.Option: The presence of genetically identical B. pseudomallei strains (PFGE Type Ia, ST58) in both aerosols and human cases, combined with northwesterly wind patterns carrying aerosols from a 5 km distant source field during the typhoon season, supports the plausibility of airborne transmission across geographic separation

The cropped field harboring a high B. pseudomallei isolation rate was located 5 km away from the core region, with the clusters of melioidosis cases in the northwest direction.

86.Rationale: This option integrates multiple lines of evidence: 1) Molecular typing showing identical strains in both environmental aerosols and human cases, 2) Geographic evidence showing the source field is 5 km upwind of the core region during typhoon season, and 3) Meteorological evidence showing northwesterly winds during this period. This combination of genetic, geographic, and meteorological evidence provides the strongest support for the airborne transmission hypothesis across geographic separation.

Evidence: In the superficial layers (<10 cm), the monthly isolation rates of B. pseudomallei were 22±2.8%-34±2.8% from July to October and 0±0%-10±2.8% during the other months.

86.Rationale: This option actually contradicts the study's findings. While the high isolation rate in superficial soil layers is mentioned, the study specifically demonstrates that melioidosis cases are clustered in urban areas (the core region) rather than in the rural fields where the high isolation rates are found. The study provides evidence that airborne transmission is necessary to explain this geographic pattern of disease.

Evidence: The B. pseudomallei-specific DNA concentration in aerosols was associated with the incidence of melioidosis and the appearance of a northwesterly wind during the typhoon season.
Changes in the B. pseudomallei-specific DNA concentration in Site G were related to the melioidosis incidence (lag 0 week, p<0.05; lag 1 week, p = 0.067).
86.Rationale: While this option correctly identifies a correlation between aerosol DNA concentration and disease incidence, it lacks the critical genetic evidence that would confirm the aerosolized DNA actually originated from the source field and could cause disease in humans. The study provides more compelling evidence through molecular typing that directly links the environmental and clinical isolates.

87.Option: The introduction of DDT and other residual chemicals into residential areas led to widespread mortality of adult Aedes aegypti, preventing population recovery after seasonal declines
Evidence: The use of DDT in sewers, garbage tips and commercial premises may have contaminated many cryptic and subterranean sites.

There are no government records of DDT being used in residential buildings and no suggestion that DDT was commonly used indoors (where Ae. aegypti commonly rests [25]). It was unlikely used in rainwater tanks as authorities were aware of its negative health effects [114].

87.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that there is no evidence DDT was used in residential areas or against Ae. aegypti specifically. The use of DDT was limited to sewers, garbage tips, and commercial premises, and there are no records of its use in residential buildings where Ae. aegypti would rest. Therefore, DDT cannot be credited as a primary factor in the species' elimination from Brisbane.

87.Option: The decline in dengue cases after 1943 reduced the incentive for continued mosquito surveillance, leading to a failure to detect and control residual populations

By 1952 health authorities understood that Ae. aegypti had been “effectively reduced in numbers to the point where it was no longer a capable link in the chain of [dengue] transmission” [98]. However the elimination campaign continued to “be vigorously waged” [99] in 1964, and by 1966 the species had been “virtually eliminated” [100].
87.Rationale: The passage indicates that mosquito surveillance and control efforts actually increased after 1943, not decreased. The city council intensified its efforts during the post-war period, with a significant increase in house-to-house surveys. The decline in dengue cases did not reduce surveillance but rather was a result of the successful elimination campaign. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

Evidence: Improvements to living standards.Broad social trends and improved living standards after the Second World War improved conditions within the domestic dwelling and outdoor yard areas.

87.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges that improvements in living standards contributed to the reduction of larval habitat, it does not state that this was the primary factor in the elimination of Ae. aegypti. The text emphasizes that the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks and the enforcement of regulations were the key drivers. The improvement in living standards is described as a contributing factor but not the most likely one. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

87.Option: The removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks as persistent larval habitat disrupted the species' ability to survive climatic stressors and maintain population continuity
Evidence: The removal of large domestic water storage containers (rainwater tanks) and their replacement with reticulated water has been suggested as the principle reason for the elimination of Ae. aegypti in the city [29, 45].
The specific decline in non-compliant rainwater tanks from the early 1930s appears to coincide with a decline in Ae. aegypti presence and a reduction in dengue transmission.

The removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks acting as permanent larval habitat that played an important role in the process of elimination.
By 1952 health authorities understood that Ae. aegypti had been “effectively reduced in numbers to the point where it was no longer a capable link in the chain of [dengue] transmission” [98].
87.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks was a key factor in the elimination of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane. 88.Rationale: The passage states that itraconazole was not used for SARS-CoV-2 treatment in this cohort, and while it mentions a potential protective effect in vitro, it does not provide evidence that itraconazole treatment during SARS-CoV-2 infection influenced outcomes in these patients. Therefore, this is not the strongest host-related factor for severe outcomes in this study.

89.Option: Perception of disease chronicity, as patients who view ZCL as long-lasting report higher emotional distress
Evidence: The inter-correlation matrix for multiple IPQ-R domains (Table 3) showed that the domains do not all measure the same constructs, because not all of them are inter-correlated.

The matrix showed further that the 'identity' domain has a negative correlation with the 'curability' domain (r = -0.35, p<0.05) and a positive correlation with the 'coherence' domain (r = 0.41, p<0.01).

89.Rationale: The number of facial scars is associated with stigma and stress, but the passage indicates that these correlations are moderate and not the strongest predictor of the interpretive framework. Hence, variables such as 'age' and 'education level' have to be monitored closely, for they may partly explain why there is a variance in the IPQ-R results.

89.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that higher education is associated with greater cognitive and emotional sensitivity to the disease. Evidence: The correlation analysis shows that high education level and high knowledge levels are associated with greater cognitive sensitivity (perception of consequences) and emotional sensitivity (emotional representations).

89.Rationale: While higher knowledge is associated with greater awareness of disease consequences, the passage indicates that the level of education is the stronger predictor of the interpretive framework. The total knowledge score is mentioned in the context of its association with education level, but it is not highlighted as the most significant independent predictor.

Evidence: The results showed that anticipation and avoidance of stress is strongly correlated with the scores on 'Consequences', 'Coherence' and 'Emotional representation.'

These results suggest that the more a person looks at the world anticipating segregation and rejection, the more they perceive ZCL as a disease with harmful effects on them, the more they see it as a mysterious and incomprehensible illness, and the more they talk about suffering and emotional difficulties.
89.Rationale: Anticipation and avoidance of stress is a consequence of the interpretive framework rather than a predictor of it. The passage indicates that this factor is strongly correlated with other domains of illness perception, but it is not the primary factor that shapes the interpretive framework itself.

90.Option: The consistent increase in seropositivity with age across all age groups supports the conclusion that antibody responses reflect cumulative lifetime exposure rather than recent exposure
Evidence: Seropositive rate increase with age in both study sites but was more pronounced in Mazán
The proportion of seropositive individuals was significantly higher in participants from Mazán than Iquitos in all age groups
There was a notable increase in the proportion seropositive people aged > 15 years in both study sites
90.Rationale: The consistent increase in seropositivity with age is more indicative of cumulative lifetime exposure rather than recent exposure. The passage explicitly states that seropositivity increases with age, which supports the idea of cumulative exposure rather than recent exposure.

Evidence: The seroprevalence of P. vivax was higher in the riverine communities of Mazán than in the peri-urban communities of Iquitos
The distribution of seropositive cases did not show a spatial structure in communities of Iquitos but did it in Mazán

90.Rationale: The fact that seropositivity was higher in individuals with outdoor occupations supports the conclusion that antibody responses reflect long-term occupational exposure rather than recent infection. This is because outdoor occupations such as logging, farming, and fishing are associated with increased exposure to mosquitoes and thus higher risk of malaria infection over time. The passage explicitly states that outdoor occupation is a risk factor for P. vivax seropositivity in Mazán, which is consistent with long-term occupational exposure.

90.Option: The use of a Random Forest algorithm to classify seropositivity based on combined antibody responses ensures that the markers detect only recent exposure and not cumulative immunity
Evidence: The seropositivity classifier was a Random Forests based classification algorithm previously validated in low P. vivax transmission contexts
The diagnostic target selected was 80% sensitivity and 80% specificity
90.Rationale: The use of a Random Forest algorithm to classify seropositivity based on combined antibody responses does not ensure that the markers detect only recent exposure and not cumulative immunity. The passage explicitly states that the algorithm was validated in low transmission settings and that the diagnostic target was 80% sensitivity and 80% specificity. However, it also notes that in high transmission settings, seropositivity may be due to either recent infection or cumulative lifetime exposure, which limits the utility of these serological markers as individual-level markers of exposure in high transmission settings.

90.Option: Higher seropositivity rates among children under 15 years in areas with recent transmission reduction suggest recent exposure, as their limited lifetime exposure reduces the likelihood of cumulative immunity
Evidence: The highest seropositive rates in participants aged <15 years were observed in Gamitanacocha (0.72, 95% CI 0.50–0.88) in Mazán, and Santo Tomás (0.21, 95% CI 0.13–0.31) in Iquitos
In areas that after long periods of moderate-to-high transmission rapidly goes to low transmission, antibodies remain at high levels for several years after transmission has been reduced
However, our study showed that in areas where transmission has shifted from high-moderate to low levels like Iquitos and Mazán, SEMs are best suited to detect recent exposure in young people
90.Rationale: Higher seropositivity rates among children under 15 years in areas with recent transmission reduction suggest recent exposure, as their limited lifetime exposure reduces the likelihood of cumulative immunity. This is because children have not had enough time to accumulate multiple exposures over their lifetime, so high seropositivity rates in this age group are more likely to indicate recent exposure rather than cumulative immunity. 91.Option: The timing of surveillance activities must be synchronized with the peak transmission season to maximize detection of residual infection
Evidence: Mosquitoes were collected with CDC gravid traps (Model 1712, John W. Hock Company, Gainesville, FL) with liquid bait that attracts Cx. quinquefasciatus [4,16]. The traps were placed outdoors in shaded areas adjacent to houses after obtaining consent from the residents. Traps were set to collect mosquitoes from dusk to dawn for 1 to 3 nights.
The mosquito surveys were performed between December 2013 and September 2014. The long duration was due to administrative issues and not because of time requirements for the work.
91.Rationale: While timing of surveillance is important for maximizing detection, the passage emphasizes that the study was conducted over a long period (December 2013 to September 2014) and still found significant results. The critical issue highlighted in the passage is not the timing of surveillance but the spatial distribution and homogeneity of evaluation units. The study's success in detecting persistent transmission despite a long collection period suggests that timing alone is not the most critical factor.

91.Option: The spatial homogeneity of evaluation units must be preserved to prevent dilution of transmission signals in surveillance data
Evidence: The results shown are the mean and SD of 30 separate randomly selected samples of 30 EAs drawn from the entire district. The results show that the estimates were essentially the same whether mosquitoes were sampled from 316, 153, or 70 trap locations. More importantly, the results show that the parasite DNA rate in the combined EU was lower and intermediate between the rates observed in the coastal and inland EUs. Although the filarial DNA rate in mosquitoes from the district wide EU exceeded the provisional target of 0.25%, the diluting effect of results from the inland areas is clear (the MLE for the coastal EU and for the entire district were 0.67% vs. 0.35%, respectively). This emphasizes the importance of creating EU’s so that transmission risks within them are as uniform as practicable [23].
91.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that combining areas with different transmission risks (e.g., coastal and inland EUs) dilutes the transmission signal, making it harder to detect persistent infection. The study emphasizes the importance of maintaining spatial homogeneity in evaluation units to avoid this dilution. This is directly supported by the evidence provided, which shows that the combined EU results were less sensitive to persistent transmission compared to the more homogeneous coastal EU.

91.Option: The sensitivity of molecular xenomonitoring must be maintained across all regions to ensure accurate detection of persistent transmission
Evidence: MX results were more sensitive than TAS for detecting persistent W. bancrofti in Sri Lanka [4].
MX is more sensitive than night blood surveys for detecting persistent W. bancrofti activity in this study area.
91.Rationale: While the passage does mention that MX is more sensitive than other methods, it does not state that maintaining sensitivity across all regions is the most critical factor. The key issue highlighted is the spatial distribution of evaluation units and the dilution effect when combining areas with different transmission risks. Sensitivity is important, but the passage emphasizes the need for spatial homogeneity as the critical factor.

91.Option: The representativeness of the sampled population must reflect the overall demographic structure of the region to avoid biased estimates
Evidence: The study was designed to collect and test 300 pools of 25 mosquitoes per pool collected from 300, 150, and 75 HH locations.
PHM maps, census data and voter registries were used to identify the HH locations in each of these areas.
91.Rationale: The passage describes a systematic sampling approach using census data and voter registries to select households, which suggests an effort to ensure representativeness. However, the critical issue highlighted in the passage is not demographic representativeness but the spatial homogeneity of evaluation units. The study's findings about the dilution effect when combining areas with different transmission risks is more central to the interpretation of surveillance data.

91.Option: The number of mosquito pools tested must be sufficient to achieve statistical power for detecting low-level transmission
Evidence: Table 2 shows that similar results were obtained when similar numbers of mosquitoes were tested in 300 pools that were collected from approximately 75, 150, or 300 sampling locations with 4, 2 or 1 pool per site, respectively.
Results in Table 3 also emphasize the importance of using EUs with moderate population sizes that do not mix areas with vastly different risks for persistent W. bancrofti.
91.Rationale: The passage indicates that similar results were obtained with different numbers of mosquito pools, suggesting that the number of pools is not the most critical factor. The key issue is the spatial homogeneity of evaluation units, as combining areas with different transmission risks can dilute the signal. The study's findings show that even with fewer pools, the results remained consistent when the evaluation units were homogeneous.

92.Rationale: The passage repeatedly emphasizes the importance of trust and rapport with healthcare providers. There is not much support to help us. Even finding ways to make ends meet for our children, maybe there should be some sort of support like vouchers for families.

I get monthly injections now. I don’t mind getting them. They’re sort of just there. If I don’t want to end up like I did in 2018 [undergoing heart surgery], then I might as well just keep taking the injection.

I get monthly injections now. I don’t mind getting them. They’re sort of just there. If I don’t want to end up like I did in 2018 [undergoing heart surgery], then I might as well just keep taking the injection.

For us, with our community and being an Islander, that age [18 years] means nothing. That support will always be there. Either me or my sister will continue …to take him to his appointments [to have BPG injected].

93.Option: The fact that streptomycin resistance was detected in a pneumonic plague patient during an outbreak suggests that the resistance emerged in the natural reservoir and was transmitted to humans.
Evidence: The epidemiological investigation indicated that the Patient C took care of Patient B (primary pneumonic plague) and unfortunately got infection.

Considering their clinical symptoms and treatment, as well as the genomic phylogenetic relationship in associated strains, we inferred that the high level of resistance by a mutation did not originate from the local reservoir of M. himalayana or previous plague outbreaks, because no such mutation in rpsL occurred in strains isolated from M. himalayana or human cases in adjacent counties.

93.Option: The presence of the novel plasmid pS96127 in S19960127, which is also found in streptomycin-susceptible strains, suggests that the plasmid may have carried the rpsL mutation and was transferred during the outbreak.

93.Option: The high-level streptomycin resistance in S19960127, with an MIC of 4,096 mg/L, is consistent with resistance observed in other Y. pestis strains from Madagascar, suggesting a shared evolutionary origin.

110.Option: Sequential dose escalation with placebo-controlled cohorts and balanced baseline characteristics
Evidence: Eight cohorts of eight subjects each were enrolled and completed the study. In each cohort, six subjects received UV-4 as the hydrochloride; two received placebo.

Subjects were excluded from study participation if they had a history of allergy to the iminosugar chemical class, creatinine clearance <90 mL/min (based on Cockcroft-Gault equation), proteinuria >30 mg/dL or abnormalities in physical examination suggestive of conditions that posed an increased risk to the subject (e.g., cardiovascular, pulmonary, endocrine, immune or other body system likely to pose a risk to the subject).
Only subjects who met all inclusion and none of the exclusion criteria prior to administration of the investigational product were enrolled into the study (no exemptions were granted).

Subjects were considered healthy based on medical history, physical exam, vital signs, 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG), clinical laboratory tests (hematology, blood chemistry, urinalysis), viral serology, and drug and alcohol screening.
Male and female subjects of childbearing potential were required to use adequate contraception over the course of the clinical trial.
Placebo and active groups were of similar composition for gender, age, race and ethnicity, weight, and body mass index.
110.Rationale: The sequential dose escalation design with placebo-controlled cohorts allows for incremental testing of higher doses only if lower doses are deemed safe. The inclusion of placebo groups in each cohort provides a direct comparison to assess whether observed adverse events are drug-related. The balanced baseline characteristics (similar gender, age, race, ethnicity, weight, and BMI between active and placebo groups) help ensure that any differences in adverse events can be more confidently attributed to the drug rather than baseline differences. The exclusion of subjects with potential risk factors and the use of strict inclusion/exclusion criteria further enhance the ability to detect true drug-related safety signals while minimizing confounding.

This suggests that the consistent production of IgG2a antibodies is a key factor in the difference in long-term protection between the two vaccines.

Evidence: KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination induced the production of KSAC-specific IFN-γ+ CD4+ T cells, and the relative size of this cell fraction was maintained after a challenge with L. major-infected sand flies (Fig. 3A).

KSAC-immunized mice generate a pool of effector memory CD4+IFN-γ+ T cells specific to KSAC that was efficiently stimulated with SLA both before and after a sand fly challenge.
123.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the sustained presence of antigen-specific effector memory T cells is a critical factor in the difference in long-term protection between the two vaccines. The KSAC+GLA-SE vaccine induced a sustained population of effector memory T cells that were capable of rapid reactivation upon challenge, as evidenced by the production of IFN-γ. In contrast, the L110f+GLA-SE vaccine did not maintain this sustained presence of effector memory T cells, leading to reduced long-term protection. This suggests that the sustained presence of these T cells is a key factor in the difference in long-term protection between the two vaccines.

The KSAC results reported here demonstrate that the synthetic TLR4 agonist GLA can be a powerful tool to direct a shift from Th2 to a Th1-type response, necessary to combat a vector-transmitted L. major infection.
123.Rationale: The passage indicates that the innate immune response induced by vector transmission can actually promote parasite establishment, rather than prevent it. The vaccines' effectiveness is attributed to the adaptive immune response (Th1 response and effector memory T cells) rather than the innate immune response. The KSAC+GLA-SE vaccine's success is linked to its ability to induce a Th1 response, not a rapid innate immune response. Therefore, the ability to prevent early parasite establishment by inducing a rapid innate immune response is not the factor that explains the difference in long-term protection between the two vaccines.

124.Option: The dominance of the dog-associated China I lineage suggests that wildlife reservoirs have been eliminated from the epidemic cycle in China
Evidence: The two major lineages also show differences in their host composition (Figure 4). Conversely, for the China II, III and IV lineages, a larger proportion of isolates were collected from wildlife (Table 1). No wildlife isolates were collected for lineages China V and VI, but the total number of isolates were small for both these clades.

Pairwise comparison of the China I, II, III and IV lineages using the Fisher's Exact test revealed that China I had a distinct host composition from China II (P = 2.74×10−9), whereas the composition of China II and China III (Cosmopolitan) were indistinguishable (P = 1). 124.Rationale: The evidence shows that while the China I lineage is primarily associated with dogs, wildlife reservoirs are still present for other lineages (China II, III, IV). Therefore, wildlife reservoirs are not eliminated but are associated with other lineages.

Conversely, for the China II, III and IV lineages, a larger proportion of isolates were collected from wildlife (Table 1).

The significant differences in the host range of the lineages is also informative. China I isolates are almost exclusively from dogs, domestic animals and human cases, whereas China II, III and IV contains a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates; it is only recently that China I strains have been isolated in wildlife.
The rapid emergence of China I indicates that, although it was present in previous epidemics, this lineage is responsible for the current epidemic.

The expansion of the epidemic into low incidence regions is a cause for concern. Prior to 2007, there were less than 10 human rabies cases reported in these regions in the last 10 years (Table S3). Since then, the number of human cases in these regions has increased as the number of cases have begun to decrease at a national level [23]. New strains isolated in 2011 in Shanxi, Inner Mongolia and Ningxia are genetically close I-A8 strains [23], indicating that this lineage is already becoming established throughout the low incidence regions and is beginning to expand into very low incidence regions.
124.Rationale: The evidence supports the inference that domestic dogs are the primary driver of the current epidemic, as the China I lineage is almost exclusively associated with dogs and has expanded geographically. In contrast, earlier epidemics were dominated by lineages (China II, III, IV) that are associated with wildlife. The text explicitly states that China I is the dominant lineage in the current epidemic and that it has replaced other lineages that were more prominent in earlier waves. The shift from wildlife-associated lineages to the dog-associated China I lineage is a key finding of the study.

China I isolates are almost exclusively from dogs, domestic animals and human cases, whereas China II, III and IV contains a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates; it is only recently that China I strains have been isolated in wildlife.
The rapid emergence of China I indicates that, although it was present in previous epidemics, this lineage is responsible for the current epidemic.

The expansion of the epidemic into low incidence regions is a cause for concern. Prior to 2007, there were less than 10 human rabies cases reported in these regions in the last 10 years (Table S3). Since then, the number of human cases in these regions has increased as the number of cases have begun to decrease at a national level [23]. New strains isolated in 2011 in Shanxi, Inner Mongolia and Ningxia are genetically close I-A8 strains [23], indicating that this lineage is already becoming established throughout the low incidence regions and is beginning to expand into very low incidence regions.

Therefore, the claim that the shift is due to increased dog populations is not supported by the evidence.

Evidence: In Culex quinquefasciatus inoculated simultaneously with CxFV Izabal and WNV, midgut and head tissues became infected with both viruses, demonstrating a potential for physical interaction between CxFV Izabal and WNV (Figs. 7,8). The presence in co-infected specimens suggests that co-infection may facilitate CxFV Izabal's access to salivary glands, but this does not explain the lack of interference between CxFV Izabal and WNV.

125.Option: The lack of interference is due to the fact that CxFV Izabal replicates in mosquito cells without causing cytopathic effects, preserving cellular resources for WNV replication
Evidence: CxFV Izabal caused evident cytopathic effects (CPE) in C6/36 cells, completely destroying the cell monolayer by 8 days post infection (DPI) following inoculation at an MOI of 0.1. In Cx. quinquefasciatus Sebring mosquitoes exposed to CxFV Izabal by intrathoracic inoculation, CxFV Izabal reached a peak titer of 4.3 log10 pfu/mosquito approximately 8 DPI. Mosquitoes were not susceptible to CxFV Izabal infection following oral exposure (Fig. 3).

As suggested by Hoshino et al. [10], CPE observed in C6/36 cells may be the result of an unnatural association between this Culex-derived virus and Aedes-derived cell line since CxFV apparently replicates avirulently in its mosquito host.
125.Rationale: The option incorrectly states that CxFV Izabal replicates without causing cytopathic effects. The passage explicitly states that CxFV Izabal caused cytopathic effects in C6/36 cells, and suggests that this may be due to the unnatural association with the Aedes-derived cell line. In its natural Culex host, CxFV is described as replicating avirulently, but this is not the reason given for the lack of interference with WNV.

125.Option: CxFV Izabal and WNV likely use different cellular receptors, preventing competition for entry and enabling co-infection without interference

CxFV may target and replicate in different mosquito tissues than WNV or other flaviviruses. In Culex quinquefasciatus inoculated simultaneously with CxFV Izabal and WNV, midgut and head tissues became infected with both viruses, demonstrating a potential for physical interaction between CxFV Izabal and WNV (Figs. 7,8). However it is unclear if these tissue tropisms would be the same for mosquitoes naturally-infected with CxFV or if infection of these tissues is an artifact of inoculation, or inoculation simultaneously with WNV.
125.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that CxFV Izabal and WNV use different cellular receptors. In fact, it suggests that both viruses can infect the same tissues when co-infected, indicating potential for receptor overlap. The lack of interference is not attributed to different receptors but rather to other factors like low sequence homology or immune evasion.

RNAi is a mechanism by which invertebrates respond to viral infection through the specific recognition and degradation of viral mRNA sequences by virus-derived small interfering RNAs (viRNA) [34]–[38]. However, our data demonstrate that prior infection with CxFV Izabal does not interfere with WNV replication when the viruses are inoculated simultaneously, or when mosquitoes are exposed to WNV one week following inoculation with CxFV Izabal.
125.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that prior infection with CxFV Izabal does not interfere with WNV replication, contradicting the claim that a broad-spectrum antiviral state is induced. The study design is not cited as the reason for the lack of detection; rather, the data directly show no interference.

125.Option: Low sequence homology between CxFV Izabal and WNV limits the effectiveness of RNA interference-mediated antiviral defense
Evidence: RNAi is a highly sequence-specific mechanism with little tolerance to mismatches between the viRNA trigger and mRNA target sequences [39], and the nucleotide sequence identity between CxFV and other vertebrate-infecting flaviviruses is relatively low. Kim et al. [13] reported between 25 and 52% nucleotide sequence homology between CxFV (TX24518) and WNV among structural and non-structural genes. Similarly, Hoshino et al. [10] reported 17–25% sequence identity for structural proteins and 17–40% identity among non-structural proteins between their Japanese isolate of CxFV and other flaviviruses.
Therefore, any interference of CxFV Izabal viRNAs with WNV was probably minimal.
125.Rationale: The passage directly supports this option by explaining that the low sequence homology between CxFV Izabal and WNV limits the effectiveness of RNA interference. This is a well-established mechanism in virology where sequence specificity is crucial for RNAi to function. The low homology means that viRNAs produced by CxFV Izabal would not effectively target WNV RNA, thus preventing interference.

126.Option: Attachment of L. interrogans to E-cadherin on epithelial cells facilitates translocation across the intestinal barrier, promoting systemic spread
Evidence: The expression of cadherins in cell monolayers that efficiently bind Leptospira was assessed by immunoblot analysis. The multiple bands observed in HMEC-1, HEp-2 and HK-2 cells suggest the presence of more than one species of cadherin in these cell lines. The band observed in Caco2BBE cell lysate was smaller in size compared to the bands observed from the rest of the cell lines. To determine the expression of specific cadherin receptors, we probed the blots with antibodies against E-cadherin, VE-cadherin and N-cadherin. VE-cadherin expression was observed in both endothelial cell lines examined, but receptor production was more robust in EA.hy926 than HMEC-1 at post-confluence. Among the three epithelial cell lines observed to bind L. interrogans, Caco2BBE was the only one in which we were able to detect E-cadherin production.

We were not able to reduce L. interrogans binding to Caco2BBE cells with anti-E-cadherin antibodies. This may either be due to the robust expression of the receptor or additional L. interrogans-receptor interactions may minimize the effects of a single antibody, consistent with the similar results obtained using the anti-VE-cadherin antibody in EA.hy926 cells.
126.Rationale: While the study does show that L. interrogans can bind to E-cadherin on epithelial cells like Caco2BBE, the evidence does not directly support that this interaction facilitates translocation across the intestinal barrier. The study notes that anti-E-cadherin antibodies failed to inhibit binding to Caco2BBE cells, suggesting other receptors or mechanisms may be involved. Additionally, the study focuses more on endothelial cell interactions as the primary interface for dissemination.

N-cadherin is also the predominant cadherin in the renal proximal tubules but is not expressed in other nephron segments.
126.Rationale: The study mentions that N-cadherin is present in renal tubule cells, but it does not provide direct evidence that L. interrogans interacts with N-cadherin to enable persistent colonization or release into the bloodstream. The focus of the study is on VE-cadherin and its role in endothelial cell interactions, not on N-cadherin's role in renal colonization.

Finally, as depicted in Figure 4D, increasing numbers of L. interrogans were incubated with a constant amount of VE-cadherin. Maximal bacterial adherence was observed when 1×104 leptospires were incubated with 0.03 µM receptor. The dose-dependent and saturable binding of L. interrogans to VE-cadherin indicates that the receptor-bacteria interaction is specific. In addition, L. interrogans bound efficiently to ICAM-2, and to fibronectin, which was used as a positive control. Attachment to the integrins was negligible, further demonstrating that L. interrogans-cadherin interactions are specific.
126.Rationale: The study shows that L. interrogans binds to fibronectin, but it is used as a positive control to demonstrate the specificity of cadherin interactions. 126.Option: Binding of L. interrogans to VE-cadherin disrupts endothelial integrity, enabling bacterial dissemination through the vascular barrier

By transwell migration assay, our group previously demonstrated that pathogenic L. interrogans but not the saprophytic L. biflexa caused disruption of the endothelial monolayer resulting in increased transmigration of bacteria.

As an endothelial junctional protein, VE-cadherin plays a major role in maintaining the barrier properties of the vasculature. It is tempting to speculate that VE-cadherin plays a critical role for the L. interrogans to attach to the vasculature, resulting in endothelial damage and facilitating the escape of the pathogen from the bloodstream into different tissues during disseminated infection.

We postulate that the binding of L. interrogans to host cells through receptors such as cadherins does not lead to intracellular invasion, but instead, in endothelial cells, activates a signaling cascade that results in cytoskeleton rearrangement or receptor recycling. Both of these events can lead to a loss in endothelial integrity, which corresponds to the endothelial disruption observed in vitro and to hemorrhagic events of leptospirosis that occur in vivo.
126.Rationale: The study provides direct evidence that L. interrogans binds to VE-cadherin on endothelial cells, and this interaction disrupts endothelial integrity. The evidence includes specific binding experiments, inhibition studies with antibodies, and previous transwell migration assays showing endothelial monolayer disruption. These findings directly support the role of endothelial cells as a critical interface for bacterial dissemination during early infection.

Instead, the role of ICAM-2 in leptospirosis is associated with the recruitment of inflammatory cells, which may contribute to pathology rather than clearance. The evidence does not support the claim that ICAM-2 binding limits dissemination.

128.Option: The exclusive association of TcV and TcVI with domestic cycles in Southern Cone countries, despite their absence in similar environments in North and Central America, suggests a region-specific ecological barrier limiting their spread
Evidence: TcV and TcVI have never been identified in US in 148 determinations, nor in Central America in 120 cases. In contrast, in South America, these DTUs together have frequently been identified in several countries, Argentina (76.9%), Bolivia (44.6%), Chile (28.6%) and Paraguay (55.2%)—but very rarely in others such as in Colombia (1.1%), Ecuador (3.3%), and Brazil (1.5%).

The two TcV and TcVI DTUs include strains derived from the hybridization of TcII and TcIII strains [9].
