Both DTUs are clearly associated with domestic cycles since only 10.5% of them are identified in hosts from wild cycles. They are identified in some Didelphimorphia and in different species of rodents but only in the Southern Cone countries and Bolivia.
128.Rationale: The evidence shows that TcV and TcVI are predominantly found in domestic cycles in Southern Cone countries and are absent in similar environments in North and Central America. This pattern suggests a region-specific ecological barrier that limits their spread beyond certain areas. The text also notes that these DTUs are associated with domestic cycles and have not been identified in other regions with similar environments, indicating a specific ecological or host-related barrier.

128.Option: The consistent detection of TcII in domestic cycles across South America, despite its absence in North and Central America, suggests a host immune barrier that prevents its establishment in human populations outside South America
Evidence: TcII was much more rarely identified (9.6% of overall DTUs identified). It was not identified in Central America out of 120 identifications, and only 13 identifications were reported from North America out of 459 (2.8%). Eight of these 13 TcII were found in Mexico, four in T. dimidiata captured in domestic cycles in the state of Veracruz [29] and four in Meccus pallidipennis collected in Michoacan [30]. The five other identifications were from mice and rats captured in the immediate surroundings of the dwelling of the first described autochthonous case of T. cruzi transmission in Louisiana, near New Orleans [31, 32]. In South America, TcII presents a higher proportion, reaching 10.4% and was reported in Colombia, Surinam, Peru, Bolivia, Brazil, Argentina, Paraguay and Chile.

In the order Carnivora, TcI (37.1%) and TcIV (58.1%) were principally identified, but of the 36 TcIV samples, 35 were from Procyon lotor captured in the US, also infected with TcI (two cases) and one Nasua nasua from Brazil, both belonging to the same Procyonidae family.
128.Rationale: The text indicates that TcII is present in North America (Mexico and US) but at very low frequencies. The absence in Central America is not explained by host immunity alone, as the text does not provide evidence of immune differences affecting TcII. The low frequency in North America may be due to other factors like vector availability or host preferences rather than a host immune barrier.

129.Option: The continued occurrence of neonatal tetanus is due to the ineffectiveness of the tetanus toxoid vaccine in preventing infection, as evidenced by the lack of significant association between maternal vaccination and survival
Evidence: About a quarter of the mothers had no antenatal care during pregnancy and close to 60% of them did not receive tetanus toxoid vaccination during pregnancy (Table 2).

Administration of anti-tetanus serum to the patients did not improve survival too. This could be due to the challenge of getting the anti-tetanus serum of good quality because of poor preservation by the outlets where most patients get it to buy.

Maternal tetanus toxoid vaccination was also not significantly associated with survival in this study unlike reports from some previous studies, this probably may be because none of the mothers in this study completed the two doses of vaccination in pregnancy.
129.Rationale: The study does not demonstrate that the tetanus toxoid vaccine is ineffective. The lack of significant association between maternal vaccination and survival is attributed to the fact that none of the mothers in the study completed the two doses of vaccination in pregnancy. The ineffectiveness of the vaccine is not established; rather, the issue is incomplete vaccination coverage. Additionally, the poor quality of anti-tetanus serum is mentioned as a separate issue, not the vaccine itself.

129.Option: Persistent transmission is driven by a convergence of structural inequities, cultural practices, and gaps in preventive healthcare access, despite overall declining incidence
Evidence: Majority of the affected babies in Nigeria are delivered either at home, faith (mission) based homes, with untrained or poorly trained traditional birth attendants as only 43% of deliveries are attended by skilled birth attendant as revealed by the 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS) report [4].
About 70% of the babies were delivered outside health facilities most likely without skilled birth attendants and under unhygienic conditions which agrees with the 2018 NDHS [4] report.
About 50% used hot water compress as cord care method and about 20% each apply shea butter or mentholatum to the cord stump.

About a quarter of the mothers in this review had no formal education while none of the mothers in this study had tertiary education, also about two thirds of the families of babies affected by NNT in this study were from lower socio-economic classes IV and V.
The low ANC attendance can also result in lack of tetanus toxoid vaccination during pregnancy or incomplete tetanus toxoid vaccination status of the mothers.

The current TT2 coverage in Nigeria is 40% which is less than the recommended 80% needed for coverage [43], while in Ekiti State the percentage of women whose last child birth was protected from tetanus was 88.2% [4].
The national average rate of Protection at birth (PAB) coverage for newborns against tetanus is 60% which is far lower than the recommended 80% needed for adequate protection [43].
129.Rationale: The study provides evidence of structural inequities (low socioeconomic status, lack of education), cultural practices (home deliveries, unhygienic cord care), and gaps in preventive healthcare access (low ANC attendance, incomplete vaccination coverage). These factors collectively contribute to the persistence of neonatal tetanus despite declining overall incidence. The convergence of these elements explains the continued occurrence of the disease in the study population.

129.Option: The persistence of neonatal tetanus is due to the high rate of home deliveries in rural areas, which are not adequately monitored by public health surveillance systems
Evidence: Majority of the affected babies in Nigeria are delivered either at home, faith (mission) based homes, with untrained or poorly trained traditional birth attendants as only 43% of deliveries are attended by skilled birth attendant as revealed by the 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS) report [4].
About 70% of the babies were delivered outside health facilities most likely without skilled birth attendants and under unhygienic conditions which agrees with the 2018 NDHS [4] report.
About 30% of the babies were delivered at home most likely under unhygienic condition too.
129.Rationale: While the high rate of home deliveries is a significant factor, the option oversimplifies the issue by focusing solely on surveillance systems. The study indicates that the problem is not just surveillance but also includes poor hygiene, lack of education, and low vaccination coverage. The persistence of neonatal tetanus is due to a combination of factors beyond just the monitoring of home deliveries.

129.Option: The persistence of neonatal tetanus is primarily due to the high prevalence of comorbidities such as anemia, hypoglycemia, and sepsis, which increase susceptibility to infection
Evidence: About 50% of the patients were deemed to have features suggestive of sepsis at presentation while 3 [10.3%] had features of autonomic dysfunction at admission. More than half of the babies had fever while on admission while one [5.3%] had hyperpyrexia. 5 [26.3%] of the babies had low blood sugar (hypoglycaemia) at presentation while 7 [36.8%] of them had anaemia at presentation.
The high mortality rate recorded may also be due to the accompanying sepsis in our patients.
129.Rationale: The study mentions the presence of comorbidities like anemia, hypoglycemia, and sepsis, but these are presented as contributing factors to mortality rather than the primary cause of the persistence of neonatal tetanus. The main drivers of the disease's persistence are related to preventive measures and healthcare access, not the comorbidities themselves.

129.Option: Neonatal tetanus persists because of the widespread use of unapproved medications such as shea butter and mentholatum, which directly introduce Clostridium tetani into the umbilical stump
Evidence: About 50% used hot water compress as cord care method and about 20% each apply shea butter or mentholatum to the cord stump.
The umbilicus was the suspected/identified portal of entry of the tetanus infection in all the babies [100%].
129.Rationale: The study indicates that the use of unapproved medications like shea butter and mentholatum contributes to the risk of infection, but it does not state that these substances directly introduce Clostridium tetani. Instead, they create conditions that allow for contamination. The persistence of neonatal tetanus is better explained by a combination of factors including poor cord care practices, but the direct introduction of the bacteria is not the primary mechanism described.

130.Option: Enhanced phagocytic activity of macrophages due to increased expression of FPR2 receptors on immune cells
Evidence: The internalization of T. cruzi in macrophages was reduced by 15-epi-LXA4
FPR2 knockout mice showed impaired reduction in cellular infiltrates and cardiac hypertrophy with AT-RvD1 treatment
AT-RvD1 activates GPR32 in addition to FPR2
130.Rationale: While FPR2 is involved in the mechanism, the passage indicates that AT-RvD1 reduces parasite load not by enhancing phagocytosis but by resolving chronic inflammation. The reduction in parasite load is attributed to the resolution of inflammation rather than direct enhancement of macrophage phagocytic activity.

130.Option: Direct inhibition of parasite replication through suppression of mitochondrial function in T. cruzi cells
Evidence: AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal properties
The reduction in parasite load is not due to direct trypanocidal activity but to the resolution of inflammation
130.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that AT-RvD1 does not have trypanocidal properties, ruling out direct inhibition of parasite replication as a mechanism.

130.Option: Resolution of chronic inflammation restores immune system function, enabling clearance of persistent parasites that otherwise evade detection due to an immunosuppressive environment
Evidence: Chronic inflammation is a determining factor in the deterioration of heart architecture and loss of functionality
AT-RvD1 reduces chronic inflammation, which may allow the immune system to clear parasites that persist due to an immunosuppressive environment
The resolution of inflammation may be advantageous as a potential therapeutic strategy for CCC, aiding in the improvement of current treatment regimens
130.Rationale: The passage explains that chronic inflammation creates an environment that allows parasites to persist. By resolving this inflammation, the immune system can more effectively clear the parasites. This is supported by the observed reduction in parasite load despite the absence of direct trypanocidal activity.

130.Option: Upregulation of parasite-specific antibodies that enhance opsonization and complement-mediated lysis
Evidence: The study focuses on the effects of AT-RvD1 on inflammation and immune cell activity, not on antibody production
No mention of antibody upregulation or complement-mediated lysis in the context of AT-RvD1 treatment
130.Rationale: The passage does not mention any role of antibodies or complement in the observed reduction in parasite load. The mechanism is attributed to the resolution of inflammation rather than antibody-mediated processes.

130.Option: Increased production of reactive oxygen species by neutrophils leading to oxidative damage of parasite membranes
Evidence: Bz could increase reactive oxygen species production at the tissue level, contributing to a pro-inflammatory environment
AT-RvD1 reduces inflammation and does not increase reactive oxygen species production
130.Rationale: The passage indicates that Bz, not AT-RvD1, may increase reactive oxygen species production. AT-RvD1 reduces inflammation and does not appear to increase ROS production, making this option inconsistent with the observed effects.

131.Option: The presence of anti-ZIKV IgG-NS1 antibodies in mothers is a stronger predictor of microcephaly than anti-DENV IgG-NS1 levels, as it reflects recent or active infection during pregnancy
Evidence: We observed that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower positivity rate for DENV IgG-NS1 in comparison with those without microcephaly (Fig 4A).
This analysis indicated that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in comparison with those without microcephaly (Fig 4C).
A ROC curve analysis indicated that the DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in samples from the mothers is robust in discriminating between both groups of children (Fig 4D).
131.Rationale: The study found that lower DENV IgG-NS1 levels and a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in mothers were associated with increased microcephaly risk. This suggests that pre-existing DENV immunity (as measured by IgG-NS1) is protective, not that anti-ZIKV IgG-NS1 is a stronger predictor. The option incorrectly reverses the relationship.

131.Option: A lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in mothers and children is indicative of increased risk for microcephaly, suggesting that pre-existing dengue immunity may protect against severe congenital Zika syndrome
Evidence: We observed that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in comparison with those without microcephaly (Fig 4C).
A ROC curve analysis indicated that the DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in samples from the mothers is robust in discriminating between both groups of children (Fig 4D).

We observed a significantly lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in children with microcephaly in comparison with non-microcephalic children (Fig 4G), and this ratio could discriminate between both groups of children (Fig 4H).
131.Rationale: The study directly found that lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratios in both mothers and children were strongly associated with microcephaly. This supports the interpretation that higher DENV IgG-NS1 (i.e., higher ratio) is protective, consistent with the hypothesis that pre-existing dengue immunity reduces microcephaly risk.

131.Option: The use of hydroxychloroquine and doxycycline in dengue patients may alter anti-NS1 antibody levels, confounding the association between DENV immunity and microcephaly risk
Evidence: The study did not mention any medications like hydroxychloroquine or doxycycline affecting antibody levels.
The study focused on antibody levels and their association with clinical outcomes without discussing confounding by treatment.
The study's statistical methods accounted for potential confounders but did not reference specific medications altering NS1 antibody levels.
131.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence that hydroxychloroquine or doxycycline were used in the study population or that they affected antibody levels. This option introduces an unmentioned confounder not supported by the data.

131.Option: Higher levels of anti-DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies in mothers are associated with increased risk of microcephaly due to enhanced antibody-dependent enhancement of Zika virus infection
Evidence: We observed that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower positivity rate for DENV IgG-NS1 in comparison with those without microcephaly (Fig 4A).

When we evaluated the relative levels of IgG-NS1, we observed significantly lower levels of DENV, but not ZIKV, antibodies in mothers of children with microcephaly relative to those without microcephaly (Fig 4B).
The study found that higher DENV IgG-NS1 levels were associated with reduced microcephaly risk, not increased risk.
131.Rationale: The study found that higher DENV IgG-NS1 levels were associated with reduced microcephaly risk, contradicting the claim that they increase risk. The option incorrectly reverses the observed association and misattributes the mechanism.

131.Option: The lack of correlation between DENV and ZIKV IgG-NS1 levels in mothers suggests that pre-existing dengue immunity has no role in modulating Zika virus outcomes in pregnancy
Evidence: We did not observe a correlation between ZIKV and DENV IgG-NS1 antibody levels in plasma from mothers (rho = −0.05; p = 0.45) or children (rho = 0.004; p = 0.95).
These results are likely related to the modest effect of prior DENV infections on the breadth and magnitude of ZIKV-reactive memory B cell responses [38].
However, the study found that lower DENV IgG-NS1 levels were associated with increased microcephaly risk, indicating a protective role despite the lack of correlation.
131.Rationale: The lack of correlation between DENV and ZIKV IgG-NS1 levels does not imply no role for DENV immunity. The study directly found that lower DENV IgG-NS1 levels were associated with increased microcephaly risk, demonstrating a protective effect of pre-existing DENV immunity despite the lack of correlation.

132.Option: The insertion of mCherry into the E3-E2 junction alters the cleavage efficiency of the furin protease, resulting in prolonged exposure of the antigen in its precursor form and enhancing antigen presentation to B cells
Evidence: The smaller sizes of E3-mCherry-E2 and mCherry-E2 than expected may due to some glycosylation difference with mCherry insertion rather than truncation within the fusion protein as the viral RNA genome was intact in gradient purified CHIKV 37997-mCherry (S1 Fig).
Insertion of mCherry between E3 and E2 might slow down the furin cleavage of E3 because the parental CHIKV 37997 virus contains very little uncleaved E3-E2 (p62) precursor.
132.Rationale: The passage mentions that mCherry insertion might slow furin cleavage of E3, but this is not presented as a mechanism for sustained antibody responses. The study actually notes that the smaller sizes of E3-mCherry-E2 and mCherry-E2 are likely due to glycosylation differences rather than cleavage issues. The focus of the study is on the surface presentation of mCherry, not on precursor form exposure.

132.Option: Repetitive, high-density display of the foreign antigen on the viral surface enhances B cell activation and drives durable, high-titer antibody responses after a single infection
Evidence: On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2).
The high density of mCherry can be easily recognized by B cells and, therefore, activate B cells efficiently.

Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry that can only be reached after boosting in mice conventionally immunized with mCherry proteins (Fig 10).
132.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the high-density, repetitive display of mCherry on the viral surface allows for efficient B cell activation. This is directly linked to the observation that a single infection with the chimeric virus induces long-lasting, high-titer antibody responses against mCherry, which is a key feature of the study's findings.

132.Option: The presence of viral RNA replication intermediates during infection strongly activates innate immune responses, leading to enhanced T cell-mediated immunity and sustained antibody production against the foreign antigen
Evidence: Virus replication generates viral RNA replication intermediates and viral RNA that strongly activate host innate immune responses, which may explain the strong immunogenicity of our replication-competent chimeric virus[29].
132.Rationale: While the passage mentions that viral RNA replication intermediates activate innate immune responses, this is presented as a possible explanation for the strong immunogenicity of the chimeric virus, not as the primary mechanism for sustained high-titer antibody responses. The study emphasizes the structural presentation of the antigen as the key factor.

132.Option: The fusion of mCherry to the E2 glycoprotein enables the antigen to be incorporated into the viral nucleocapsid core, allowing for efficient delivery to antigen-presenting cells and robust B cell activation
Evidence: The mature CHIKV particles bud at the plasma membrane and have T = 4 quasi-icosahedral symmetry with 240 copies of the E1E2 heterodimer, assembled into 80 spikes on the viral surface; 240 copies of C form an icosahedral nucleocapsid core enclosing viral genomic RNA within the lipid membrane [3].
On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2).
132.Rationale: The passage clearly states that mCherry is presented on the viral surface between spikes, not in the nucleocapsid core. The nucleocapsid core is composed of the C protein and encloses the viral RNA. The mCherry is part of the envelope glycoprotein complex, not the nucleocapsid.

132.Option: The chimeric virus's ability to persist in host tissues for extended periods provides continuous antigen exposure, which drives prolonged B cell stimulation and high-titer antibody production
Evidence: At 28 days post-infection, in the chronic phase of CHIKV infection, persistent viral RNA in ankle tissues was compared (Fig 8C).

In contrast, viral RNA in the ankles on the contralateral side of CHIKV 37997-mCherry infected mice were not significantly higher than the assay limit of detection, and viral loads in ankles on the ipsilateral side were significantly lower than those in parental CHIKV 37997 infected mice (Fig 8C).
132.Rationale: The passage indicates that CHIKV 37997-mCherry is attenuated and does not persist in host tissues to the same extent as the parental strain. The study actually shows that viral RNA levels in CHIKV 37997-mCherry infected mice are significantly lower and not detectable in contralateral ankles, contradicting the idea of extended persistence driving antibody responses.

134.Option: Fatigue is a key predictor of severe disease outcomes in individuals with a history of eye worm migration, even when microfilaremia is absent
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often. This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations (adjusted prevalence ratio [95% confidence interval]: (1.40 [1.10–1.79] for EW and 2.05 [1.56–2.69] for EWMF), paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]). In addition, individuals in the EW category reported myalgia and severe headache more often compared to LN (1.36 [1.01–1.82] and 1.33 [1.15–1.54], respectively). The frequency of the reported manifestations was similar between MF and LN subjects.

In summary, the infection states comprising participants with history of eye worm were significantly associated with the occurrence of clinical complaints. Contrarily, microfilaremic individuals reported complaints in a similar proportion as persons without signs of loiasis infection. The proportion of reported manifestations was independent of extent of microfilaremia, with exception for occurrence of eye worm.
134.Rationale: While fatigue is reported more frequently in individuals with a history of eye worm migration (EW and EWMF), the passage does not establish fatigue as a key predictor of severe disease outcomes. The study notes that individuals with a history of eye worm migration report various symptoms, but it does not specifically link fatigue to severe disease outcomes. The passage also states that microfilaremic individuals (MF) report complaints similar to those without signs of loiasis, indicating that microfilaremia itself is not associated with severe symptoms. Therefore, fatigue alone is not presented as a strong predictor of severe outcomes.

134.Option: The presence of urticaria is strongly associated with severe disease outcomes in individuals with a history of eye worm migration, independent of microfilaremia
Evidence: Comparing the 3 LP categories among each other, EW and EWMF subjects reported some manifestations more frequently than MF individuals. This was the case for myalgia (1.79 [1.08–2.97] and 1.76 [1.04–2.99], respectively) and Calabar swelling-like manifestations (1.55 [1.00–2.40] and 2.27 [1.45–3.55]). Further, EW subjects reported several disease manifestations more often than the MF subjects; this was the case for transient swellings of any body part (other swellings 1.81 [1.05–3.10]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.84 [1.48–5.47]), urticaria (2.38 [1.23–4.59]) and fatigue (1.53 [1.02–2.30]).

In summary, the infection states comprising participants with history of eye worm were significantly associated with the occurrence of clinical complaints. Contrarily, microfilaremic individuals reported complaints in a similar proportion as persons without signs of loiasis infection. The proportion of reported manifestations was independent of extent of microfilaremia, with exception for occurrence of eye worm.
134.Rationale: The presence of urticaria is reported more frequently in individuals with a history of eye worm migration (EW) compared to microfilaremic individuals (MF). However, the passage does not establish that urticaria is strongly associated with severe disease outcomes. The study notes that urticaria is more common in EW subjects, but it does not link this symptom to severe outcomes. The passage also states that microfilaremic individuals report complaints similar to those without signs of loiasis, indicating that microfilaremia itself is not associated with severe symptoms. Therefore, urticaria alone is not presented as a strong predictor of severe outcomes.

134.Option: Elevated absolute eosinophil counts are associated with high microfilarial density and predict severe disease outcomes, even in individuals without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: There was no statistical difference in hemoglobin values between LP and LN nor between the different loiasis infection states. However, LP had higher total WBC counts (median [inter quartile range]: 7.4 [5.9–8.9] vs. 6.7 [5.4–8.4] x103 leucocytes/μL, p-adj.<0.001), as well as higher absolute eosinophils/μL (1.1 [0.5–1.8] vs. 0.6 [0.25–1.3] x103, p-adj.<0.001) and relative eosinophil counts (15.0% [8.0–22.0] vs. 9.2% [4.1–17.0] of total WBCC, p-adj.<0.001) compared to LN. Differences in WBC counts and eosinophilia were also found when comparing the loiasis infection states individually to LN and among each other.

Comparing all individuals with microfilaremia (MF and EWMF) to patients with history of eye worm only (EW), the two former categories were characterized by higher absolute eosinophil (1.3 [0.8–1.85] and 1.4 [0.8–2.0] vs. 0.8 [0.34–1.6] 103/μL; p-adj. = 0.076 and 0.003) and relative eosinophil counts (18.0% [11.0–23.0] and 17.0% [12.0–24.0] vs. 13.0% [6.0–21.0]; p-adj. = 0.016 and 0.002).

The HYMF group had higher absolute eosinophil counts than HMF and LMF (1.70 [0.60–2.70] vs.1.40 [0.90–1.90] and 1.30 [0.80–1.90] 103/μL; p-adj. = 0.034 and p-adj. = 0.021), respectively (see Fig A in S1 Appendix). Of note, of the 56 individuals in the HMF and HYMF groups, none had an absolute eosinophil count within normal range (≤0.5x103/μL). Next, a simple linear regression analysis of the microfilaremic sub-population of the study, with at least 2 microfilariae per mL revealed an increase in absolute eosinophil count with every 10-fold increase in parasitemia (p-adj. = 0.012, ß-estimate: 0.17 [0.04–0.31], see Fig B in S1 Appendix).

In summary, loiasis was associated with absolute and relative eosinophilia. Extent of eosinophilia was higher in microfilaremic individuals in comparison to only eye worm positives. The level of eosinophilia was associated with the quantity of L. loa microfilaremia.

It is established that chronic eosinophilia can cause organ damage due to chronic inflammation [32]. The here described finding of an association between extent of eosinophilia and microfilaria counts, might therefore pathophysiologically explain the previously described excess mortality in highly microfilaremic individuals [13,19]. However, this theory needs further investigation to be verified or refuted.

Further, one could hypothesize that combining the subjective well-being of hyper-microfilaremic individuals and their previously described risk for excess mortality, could lead to inappropriately low health care seeking behavior in those most at risk for severe disease outcomes.
134.Rationale: Elevated absolute eosinophil counts are associated with high microfilarial density and are linked to severe disease outcomes in microfilaremic individuals. However, the passage does not state that elevated eosinophil counts predict severe outcomes in individuals without detectable microfilaremia. The study notes that individuals with a history of eye worm migration (EW) have only moderate elevation of absolute eosinophil counts, and that microfilaremic individuals (MF and EWMF) have higher eosinophilia. The association between eosinophilia and severe outcomes is specifically noted in microfilaremic individuals, not in those without detectable microfilaremia. Therefore, the option is incorrect as it incorrectly extends the association to individuals without microfilaremia.

134.Option: A history of eye worm migration is associated with neurological symptoms and fatigue, suggesting increased risk of severe disease even without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often. This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations (adjusted prevalence ratio [95% confidence interval]: (1.40 [1.10–1.79] for EW and 2.05 [1.56–2.69] for EWMF), paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]). In addition, individuals in the EW category reported myalgia and severe headache more often compared to LN (1.36 [1.01–1.82] and 1.33 [1.15–1.54], respectively). The frequency of the reported manifestations was similar between MF and LN subjects.

In summary, the infection states comprising participants with history of eye worm were significantly associated with the occurrence of clinical complaints. Contrarily, microfilaremic individuals reported complaints in a similar proportion as persons without signs of loiasis infection. The proportion of reported manifestations was independent of extent of microfilaremia, with exception for occurrence of eye worm.

Importantly, we found that neurological symptoms, including paresthesia and transient paralysis of extremities, were strongly associated with loiasis, specifically with eye worm positive loiasis and not with microfilaremia itself. While these symptoms have been described in reports on returning travelers, data on their occurrence in individuals living in endemic regions are scarce [7,28,29]. However, in the here described population these neurologic findings were quite frequent in the loiasis positive individuals and should thus be further investigated in future clinical studies.

In addition to the neurological symptoms, a range of unspecific symptoms, such as fatigue or headache, were quite common and should therefore be taken into account by clinicians as diagnostic hints for loiasis.
134.Rationale: A history of eye worm migration is associated with neurological symptoms (paresthesia, transient paralysis of extremities) and fatigue, which are indicators of increased risk of severe disease outcomes. The study explicitly states that these neurological symptoms are strongly associated with eye worm positive loiasis and not with microfilaremia itself. Additionally, the passage notes that individuals with a history of eye worm migration report fatigue and other symptoms more frequently, even in the absence of detectable microfilaremia. This supports the option as correct, as it directly links the history of eye worm migration to severe disease indicators independent of microfilaremia.

134.Option: A history of eye worm migration is associated with myalgia and severe headache, which are strong indicators of severe disease risk even without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often. This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations (adjusted prevalence ratio [95% confidence interval]: (1.40 [1.10–1.79] for EW and 2.05 [1.56–2.69] for EWMF), paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]). In addition, individuals in the EW category reported myalgia and severe headache more often compared to LN (1.36 [1.01–1.82] and 1.33 [1.15–1.54], respectively). The frequency of the reported manifestations was similar between MF and LN subjects.

In summary, the infection states comprising participants with history of eye worm were significantly associated with the occurrence of clinical complaints. Contrarily, microfilaremic individuals reported complaints in a similar proportion as persons without signs of loiasis infection. The proportion of reported manifestations was independent of extent of microfilaremia, with exception for occurrence of eye worm.
134.Rationale: While a history of eye worm migration is associated with myalgia and severe headache, the passage does not establish these symptoms as strong indicators of severe disease risk. The study notes that these symptoms are reported more frequently in individuals with a history of eye worm migration, but it does not link them to severe outcomes. The passage also states that microfilaremic individuals report complaints similar to those without signs of loiasis, indicating that microfilaremia itself is not associated with severe symptoms. Therefore, myalgia and severe headache alone are not presented as strong predictors of severe outcomes.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization with a moderate dose of the conjugate vaccine to promote both systemic memory B cells and mucosal IgA responses
Evidence: We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).

Interestingly, parenteral administration of whole cell cholera vaccine results in protection comparable to that afforded by oral immunization with killed whole cell vaccines, although the previously available parenteral vaccine required multiple administrations, frequent boosters, and was associated with significant local reaction-adverse events [35].

We also observed that higher TT-specific responses occurred in mice immunized intradermally compared to those induced in mice receiving the same vaccine dose intramuscularly. These data suggest that intradermal immunization of conjugate may not only induce mucosal responses, but may permit dose reduction [41].

Our study has a number of limitations. We did not assess memory or long lived plasma cell responses in intestinal tissue, we did not assess cross protection against Ogawa challenge, we did not quantify IgG responses in intestinal tissue, and we did not assess protection afforded by intradermal immunization directly. 141.Option: Chest pain is a key early indicator of severe pulmonary complications in paragonimiasis due to its direct association with pleural invasion by migrating larvae
Evidence: Chest pain is observed in most cases of primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP), but is less common in cases of secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (SSP) [11]. However, chest pain was identified in 57.9% of patients with pneumothorax associated with paragonimiasis, and simultaneous chest pain and dyspnea were reported in 37%. According to Yoshida A, et al. chest pain occurs when the Paragonimus larvae invades and injures the pleura, causing pneumothorax [1]. Therefore, although chest pain is less common in SSP, it is nevertheless an important clinical clue that suggests pneumothorax in SSP related to pulmonary paragonimiasis.

The mechanism of pneumothorax associated with pulmonary paragonimiasis has not been clearly elucidated. What is clear, however, is that larvae from the small intestine enter the abdominal cavity and penetrate the diaphragm, the pleura is damaged, and the larvae then migrates to the lung parenchyma, resulting in pneumothorax, which can be accompanied by pleural effusion due to an inflammatory response [15].
141.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that chest pain is a key early indicator of severe pulmonary complications in paragonimiasis. It states that chest pain occurs when Paragonimus larvae invade and injure the pleura, causing pneumothorax. This is supported by the finding that 57.9% of patients with pneumothorax associated with paragonimiasis experienced chest pain, which is a higher proportion than typically seen in secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (SSP). The passage also explains the mechanism by which chest pain is directly associated with pleural invasion by migrating larvae, making it a significant clinical feature in this population.

144.Option: Inclusion of sexual reproduction dynamics would increase the required number of treatment rounds to achieve elimination due to reduced fertility at higher worm burdens
Evidence: The effect of the introduction of SR can be understood by looking at the form of the mating probability factor, φ (See Figure 1A and equation 5). Therefore it is only when worm burdens drop below this level that SR starts to have a limiting effect on net parasite transmission within a community.

Figure 3B illustrates this effect. It shows, under annual treatment, changes over time in the mean worm burden among school-age children, both with and without sexual reproduction, for the default parameter values but with R0=2, to better illustrate conditions under which elimination is possible. Initial rounds of treatment have very similar impacts on the mean worm burden of in both the SR and non-SR context. However, in the case of SR, the effect of mate scarcity for worms in children means that the recovery rate is slightly reduced with respect to the non-SR regime. Over a number of treatment rounds, the effect of impaired recovery of the parasite population accumulates until it can no longer support itself and is eliminated.
144.Rationale: The passage indicates that sexual reproduction (SR) dynamics actually reduce the required number of treatment rounds to achieve elimination, not increase it. The mating probability factor φ only becomes significant when worm burdens drop below about 2, and this mate scarcity reduces recovery rates. The model shows that SR leads to elimination in 10 rounds of annual treatment rather than 15 without SR, demonstrating that SR accelerates elimination rather than requiring more rounds.

Figure 3D further illustrates this phenomenon. It shows the minimum number of treatment rounds necessary to achieve elimination as a function of R0 and the interval between treatments. Evidence: Inoculation of the amplified scMP-12 into naïve cells results in viral RNA synthesis, expression of viral proteins, including L, N and the fusion-defective Gn/Gc, and production of noninfectious VLPs containing the fusion-defective Gn/Gc.

In immunized hosts, scMP-12 undergoes single cycle replication in infected cells, resulting in the intracellular accumulation of all of the viral structural proteins and the production of noninfectious VLPs.
198.Rationale: The vaccine is able to replicate in naïve cells, but only for a single cycle. This single-cycle replication is what prevents the production of infectious virus and the risk of neuroinvasion, not an inability to replicate.

199.Option: The higher incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea in older children, particularly in the second year of life, suggests that environmental exposure increases with age and supports the role of environmental reservoirs
Evidence: The overall incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea was 31.8 cases (95% CI: 29.6, 34.2) per 100 child-years.
Incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea was higher in older age groups (incidence rate ratio: 1.75, 95% CI: 1.70, 1.81 per 6-month increase in age).
199.Rationale: While the higher incidence in older children is a key finding, this pattern is more consistent with the development of immunity over time or increased opportunity for exposure through age-related behaviors rather than direct evidence for environmental transmission. The passage does not provide evidence that environmental exposure increases with age, nor does it directly link the age pattern to environmental reservoirs.

199.Option: The strong association between Shigella and elevated myeloperoxidase levels, a marker of intestinal inflammation, indicates that environmental exposure triggers immune activation and supports environmental transmission
Evidence: Myeloperoxidase levels were 0.33 log(ng/mL) (95% CI: 0.27, 0.40) higher in stools with Shigella, and the association was greater in diarrheal stools (mean difference: 0.65, 95% CI: 0.30, 1.00 log(ng/ml)) than in non-diarrheal stools (mean difference: 0.32, 95% CI: 0.26, 0.39; p for heterogeneity: 0.08).
199.Rationale: The association with MPO indicates intestinal inflammation, which is a pathophysiological consequence of Shigella infection rather than evidence for environmental transmission. The passage does not suggest that environmental exposure triggers immune activation in a way that supports environmental transmission.

199.Option: The high prevalence of Shigella in non-diarrheal stools across all sites, including in children without clinical symptoms, suggests that asymptomatic individuals are the primary drivers of environmental transmission
Evidence: The prevalence of Shigella was 11.5% (n = 4744) overall, and was higher in diarrheal stools than non-diarrheal stools (18.4% vs. 10.1%; p<0.0001).

Almost half (611/1407, 43.4%) of children with Shigella detected in one or more non-diarrheal stools never had a diarrhea episode in which Shigella was detected, and almost two-thirds (900/1407, 64.0%) never experienced a Shigella-attributable diarrhea episode.
199.Rationale: While the high prevalence in non-diarrheal stools is notable, the passage does not provide evidence that asymptomatic individuals are the primary drivers of environmental transmission. The finding is more consistent with subclinical carriage rather than direct evidence for environmental transmission.

199.Option: Seasonal patterns in Shigella prevalence that correlate with temperature and are consistent with temperature-dependent vector activity, such as houseflies, support environmental transmission
Evidence: The seasonality of Shigella infections differed by site (Fig 2). Peak prevalence was observed in May/June in Bangladesh, June in Nepal, July/August in India, November in South Africa, and February in Tanzania. Two peaks were observed in Pakistan and Peru, and there was little seasonality in Brazil.

Among climactic factors that could explain these heterogeneities, temperature was more strongly associated with Shigella detection than rainfall, though temperature was collinear with rainfall at most sites (Fig 2, S6 Table). Higher temperature was most strongly associated with Shigella in Nepal (aRR: 2.71, 95% CI: 1.96, 3.75) and Tanzania (aRR: 1.92, 95% CI: 1.63, 2.27). Uniquely, higher rainfall was protective in Pakistan (aRR: 0.78, 95% CI: 0.61, 1.00; S6 Table).

The identification of unimproved sanitation as a risk factor alongside seasonal patterns that correlate strongly with average temperature and rainfall suggest environmental transmission pathways may be important. The seasonal patterns also support the potential implication of houseflies as a mechanical vector, as housefly population densities are seasonal and have been shown to correlate with Shigella [29].
199.Rationale: The seasonal patterns in Shigella prevalence that correlate with temperature and the explicit mention of houseflies as potential mechanical vectors directly support the hypothesis of environmental transmission. The passage states that these patterns are consistent with temperature-dependent vector activity, which is a key mechanism for environmental transmission beyond direct person-to-person contact.

199.Option: The strong association between unimproved sanitation and Shigella infection, particularly in sites with high seasonal peaks, indicates that contaminated water sources are the main route of environmental transmission
Evidence: In univariable analysis, unimproved sanitation, crowding (2+ people living in a single room), <10 years of maternal education, having 3+ live children, initiating complementary foods before 3 months, recent diarrhea, antibiotic use (particularly fluoroquinolone use), and lower anthropometric measurements prior to sample collection were also associated with higher risk of Shigella infection (Table 5). Of these variables, unimproved sanitation (aRR: 1.15, 95% CI: 1.03, 1.29), less than 10 years of maternal education (aRR: 1.14, 95% CI: 1.03, 1.26), and child WAZ at the most recent measurement prior to diarrhea (aRR: 0.91, 95% CI: 0.88, 0.95 per z-score increase in weight) were the strongest risk factors in multivariable analysis (Table 5).

The identification of unimproved sanitation as a risk factor alongside seasonal patterns that correlate strongly with average temperature and rainfall suggest environmental transmission pathways may be important.
199.Rationale: While unimproved sanitation is a significant risk factor, the passage does not provide evidence that contaminated water sources are the main route of environmental transmission. The seasonal patterns and the mention of houseflies as potential vectors suggest other environmental transmission pathways beyond water sources.

200.Option: Exposure to constant darkness after entrainment to a light/dark cycle
Evidence: The mating activity rhythm was still observed under LDDD (GLM, χ2 = 35.15, d.f. = 7, P < 0.0001) and was similar to the LD cycles with mating activity peaking at CT0-3 and CT9-12 (Fig 1B).
The mating rhythm of mosquitoes under DLDD was similar to those of mosquitoes under DL cycles (Fig 1C).
These results support the conclusion that the mating activity rhythm of Ae. albopictus is controlled by an endogenous clock and can be entrained to daily LD cycles.
The mating activity rhythm remained consistent under LDDD as well as in LD with a mating peak at CT12-15 (Fig 1D).
The mating rhythm of Cx. quinquefasciatus is also controlled by an endogenous clock and can be entrained to daily LD cycles.
200.Rationale: Exposure to constant darkness after entrainment to a light/dark cycle is a standard method to assess whether a behavior is driven by an endogenous circadian clock. If the mating rhythm persists in constant darkness (LDDD or DLDD), it indicates the behavior is governed by an internal clock rather than immediate environmental stimuli. The passage explicitly states that mating rhythms persisted in constant darkness for both Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus, supporting this conclusion.

200.Option: Assessment of mating activity during both light and dark phases under a 12:12 light/dark cycle
Evidence: The diel mating activities of Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus were measured during 12:12 LD cycles with lights on at ZT0, lights off at ZT12 (Fig 1A).
The total mating rates of Ae. albopictus were significantly higher in the daytime or subjective daytime than that at night under different conditions (GLM, all P < 0.01; Fig 1F).
In Cx. quinquefasciatus, the total mating rates were significantly higher at night than that in the daytime or subjective daytime under different conditions (GLM, all P < 0.01; Fig 1F).
200.Rationale: Assessing mating activity during both light and dark phases under a 12:12 LD cycle only shows that mating behavior is entrained to the external light/dark cycle. It does not distinguish whether the behavior is driven by the internal circadian clock or by the immediate environmental stimuli (light/dark). The passage shows that mating activity varies with the light/dark cycle, but this alone does not confirm endogenous control.

200.Option: Measurement of mating activity at multiple zeitgeber times during a single light/dark cycle
Evidence: The daily mating activities of Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus were measured at different zeitgeber times of a day (Fig 1A).
The mating activities were assessed at zeitgeber times including ZT0-3, ZT3-6, ZT6-9, and ZT9-12 for daytime and ZT12-15, ZT15-18, ZT18-21, and ZT21-24 for nighttime (Fig 1B, 1C, 1D, 1E).
200.Rationale: Measuring mating activity at multiple zeitgeber times during a single light/dark cycle provides information about the timing of mating behavior but does not distinguish between endogenous circadian control and direct environmental influence. The passage shows that mating activity varies across zeitgeber times, but this variation is consistent with entrainment to the external light/dark cycle rather than evidence of an internal clock.

200.Option: Comparison of mating activity under reversed light/dark cycles to assess phase shifts in behavior
Evidence: The mating activity rhythm appeared entrained by the DL cycles (Fig 1C).
The mating rhythm of mosquitoes under DLDD was similar to those of mosquitoes under DL cycles (Fig 1C).
The mating rhythm of Cx. quinquefasciatus under DL cycles showed that most mosquitoes mated at night and there was only a mating peak at ZT0-3 (Fig 1E).
200.Rationale: Reversing the light/dark cycle can show that the mating behavior is entrainable to external cues, but it does not distinguish between endogenous circadian control and direct environmental influence. The passage shows that mating rhythms shift with the reversed light/dark cycle, but this entrainment does not confirm that the behavior is driven by an internal clock rather than the immediate environment.

200.Option: Use of clock gene knockout mutants to assess disruption of mating rhythm under light/dark cycles
Evidence: Cas9/guide RNA-mediated clock (clk) gene knockouts affect mating activity rhythms of Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus.

The mating rate of AalclkΔ293 males was > 70% at each zeitgeber time during the day. However, there was a mating peak at ZT12-15 in dark phase, showing a delayed mating peak phase that did not occur in the WT group (Fig 2D).
CxqclkΔ98 mating activities were observed at each zeitgeber time during the daytime but not at night and the nighttime mating peak at ZT12-15 disappeared (Fig 2I).
These results indicate that the core clock gene clock (clk) in the two mosquito species have significant roles in maintaining the diel mating activity rhythms.
200.Rationale: Using clock gene knockout mutants is a direct way to assess whether the circadian clock is necessary for the observed mating rhythm. The passage shows that knocking out the clk gene in both Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus disrupted their mating rhythms under light/dark cycles, providing strong evidence that the timing of mating behavior is driven by internal circadian mechanisms rather than immediate environmental stimuli.

201.Option: The geographic distribution of CFA-positive cases in the initial TAS 1 survey can be used to predict the likelihood of transmission resurgence in future years
Evidence: However, the remaining 18 communes have had persistent LF transmission despite more than 10 years of MDA, and other communes have had an increase in antigen-positive children despite continuing to pass their TAS.
Of these, four came from the commune of Miragoane, with two residing across the street from each other in a dense urban area.

The two CFA-positive children under 7 years were also found in Miragoane, which is concerning since MDA was stopped in Nippes eight years prior and might be a further indication of ongoing transmission.
201.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that geographic distribution alone is insufficient to predict transmission resurgence. For example, Miragoane had a low baseline prevalence but later showed increased CFA positivity, indicating that initial geographic distribution does not reliably predict future outcomes. The presence of clustered cases in Miragoane after MDA cessation is interpreted as a sign of ongoing transmission, not a predictable pattern from initial TAS 1 data.

201.Option: The number of CFA-positive children identified in the initial TAS 1 survey determines the duration of post-MDA surveillance required to confirm elimination
Evidence: The EU must then pass 2 additional TAS (TAS 2 and TAS 3) over a period of 4–6 years.

Once all EUs in a country successfully pass three TAS, the country is eligible to submit a dossier to the WHO to receive formal acknowledgement of validation of the elimination of LF as a public health problem.
201.Rationale: The passage clearly states that the duration of post-MDA surveillance is fixed at 4–6 years, requiring three TAS (TAS 1, 2, and 3) regardless of the initial number of CFA-positive children in TAS 1. The number of initial positives does not determine the duration of surveillance, which is a standardized process.

201.Option: The proportion of CFA-positive children in the initial TAS 1 survey relative to the total population determines whether MDA should be resumed after a program is validated
Evidence: The WHO guidelines then state that MDA can be stopped in the EU if prevalence of LF antigenemia among children this age in the first TAS (TAS 1) is below 2% in regions where Anopheles or Culex mosquitoes are the main vectors or below 1% where Aedes mosquito species are the main vectors.

The Haiti National Program to Eliminate LF has decided to adjust the practice of combining all the Nippes Department communes for the upcoming TAS 3 and will evaluate Miragoane separately from the rest of the department.
201.Rationale: The decision to resume MDA is not based on the proportion of CFA-positive children in the initial TAS 1 survey relative to the total population but on subsequent surveillance findings, such as the presence of antigen-positive children under 7 years old after MDA cessation. The passage indicates that Miragoane's higher CFA positivity in post-MDA surveillance led to a decision to evaluate it separately, not based on initial TAS 1 proportions.

201.Option: The presence of CFA-positive children in the same geographic zone as the initial index cases in TAS 1 confirms that transmission has been interrupted and justifies program closure
Evidence: The two CFA-positive children under 7 years were also found in Miragoane, which is concerning since MDA was stopped in Nippes eight years prior and might be a further indication of ongoing transmission.

This is a change from historical data, as Miragoane had no positive LF cases during initial mapping; however, the dramatic urbanization of the commune and influx of people from rural areas might have resulted in a higher concentration of cases and thereby allowed low levels of transmission despite the pressure of MDA.
201.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the presence of CFA-positive children in the same geographic zone as initial index cases is a sign of potential ongoing transmission, not confirmation of interrupted transmission. The finding in Miragoane is described as concerning and indicative of possible ongoing transmission, contradicting the option's claim.

201.Option: The presence of antigen-positive children under 7 years old after MDA cessation indicates potential ongoing transmission, requiring re-evaluation of program success despite passing initial surveillance thresholds
Evidence: Measuring infection in children this age is relevant for LF since they are of sufficient age to develop a mature parasite but young enough for a current infection to indicate recent transmission.

The two CFA-positive children under 7 years were also found in Miragoane, which is concerning since MDA was stopped in Nippes eight years prior and might be a further indication of ongoing transmission.

Seropositivity in the youngest age group could indicate persistent or recurrent LF transmission since infection was likely acquired after MDA was stopped eight years prior to the study and it takes approximately three years to develop antibodies after initial infection.
201.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that children under 7 years old who are antigen-positive after MDA cessation are strong indicators of recent or ongoing transmission. This is because such young children would not have been exposed to the parasite before MDA cessation, making their infection a clear sign of recent transmission. The findings in Miragoane, where CFA-positive children under 7 were identified, are explicitly described as concerning and indicative of potential ongoing transmission, necessitating re-evaluation of program success despite passing initial surveillance thresholds.

202.Option: The use of high-throughput quantitative mass spectrometry enables detection of subtle protein expression differences that reflect host genetic variation
Evidence: The aims of the present study were to investigate the differences in the liver proteomes between the two mouse strains, one deemed to be susceptible, the other resistant, with both control and infected groups within each strain.

We specifically used label-free quantitative (LFQ) mass spectrometry on total protein extracts from the left liver lobes of C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca mice with and without Ascaris infection sampled on day 4 post-infection (p.i.).
202.Rationale: While high-throughput mass spectrometry is a powerful tool for detecting protein expression differences, the question specifically asks about the feature of the inbred strain that allows identification of intrinsic biological differences. The use of mass spectrometry is a methodological choice, not a feature of the inbred strain itself.

202.Option: The ability to compare proteomic profiles between infected and uninfected mice within each strain allows for the identification of infection-induced changes that may contribute to susceptibility
Evidence: The aims of the present study were to investigate the differences in the liver proteomes between the two mouse strains, one deemed to be susceptible, the other resistant, with both control and infected groups within each strain.
LFQ MS was performed on three replicates originating from the liver left lobes of C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca mice with and without Ascaris infection.
202.Rationale: Comparing infected and uninfected profiles within a strain helps identify infection-induced changes, but the question focuses on the feature of the inbred strain that allows identification of intrinsic differences. This option addresses experimental design rather than the inherent property of the inbred strain.

202.Option: The use of genetically defined inbred mouse strains with stable, opposing phenotypes enables isolation of pre-existing biological differences underlying susceptibility
Evidence: C57BL/6J are uniquely susceptible to infection with the porcine ascarid, A. suum, and larval burdens recovered from the lungs of this strain, markedly exceed those recovered from similarly infected strains of which the most resistant is the CBA/Ca mouse.
These two strains therefore represent opposite response phenotypes mimicking the extremes of predisposition detected in humans and pigs.
202.Rationale: The key feature of inbred strains is their genetic homogeneity and stable phenotypes, which allow researchers to isolate and study pre-existing biological differences. By using strains with opposing phenotypes (susceptible vs. resistant), the study can identify intrinsic differences that underlie susceptibility to parasitic infection.

202.Option: The use of a single time point post-infection (day 4) ensures that all biological responses are captured at a consistent stage of larval migration, minimizing variability
Evidence: The hepatic lobe and time-point choice were based on the study of Dold et al. who demonstrated relatively high and equivalent larval numbers in C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca left lobes at day 4 p.i., a time point at which they also observed the onset of a differential inflammatory response to infection between the two mouse strains.
LFQ MS was performed on three replicates originating from the liver left lobes of C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca mice with and without Ascaris infection sampled on day 4 post-infection (p.i.).
202.Rationale: Using a single time point reduces variability in larval migration stages, but this is an experimental design choice, not a feature of the inbred strain itself. The question asks about the feature of the inbred strain that allows identification of intrinsic differences.

202.Option: The availability of multiple inbred mouse strains with varying degrees of resistance allows for a dose-response analysis of susceptibility across a spectrum of phenotypes
Evidence: C57BL/6J are uniquely susceptible to infection with the porcine ascarid, A. suum, and larval burdens recovered from the lungs of this strain, markedly exceed those recovered from similarly infected strains of which the most resistant is the CBA/Ca mouse.
These two strains therefore represent opposite response phenotypes mimicking the extremes of predisposition detected in humans and pigs.
202.Rationale: The study uses two strains with opposing phenotypes (C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca), not multiple strains with varying resistance. The option suggests a dose-response analysis across a spectrum, which is not the case in this study.

203.Option: The presence of early thrombin generation with both venoms suggests that thrombosis is inevitable in all Bothrops envenomations, regardless of species-specific differences.
Evidence: TGA showed a decrease in lag-time and time to peak and an increase in thrombin peak at H3 with the two venoms while ETP was only increased with B. atrox venom.
Thrombosis may be due to coagulation-induced inflammatory activity of the venom.
However, thrombosis is not specific of B. lanceolatus envenomation but may rarely occur with B. atrox bite.
203.Rationale: The passage indicates that while both venoms induce early thrombin generation, thrombosis is not inevitable in all Bothrops envenomations. The text explicitly states that thrombosis is not specific to B. lanceolatus envenomation but may rarely occur with B. atrox bites. The divergent clinical outcomes (hemorrhage vs. thrombosis) are attributed to species-specific differences and other factors like inflammation and fibrinogen dynamics, not inevitability.

203.Option: The divergent clinical outcomes in Bothrops envenomation may stem from early coagulopathy followed by either persistent consumption (leading to bleeding) or recovery with inflammation-driven hypercoagulability (leading to thrombosis)
Evidence: Our global investigation of hemostasis suggest that mechanisms involved in Bothrops envenomation-induced bleeding and thrombosis are not distinct but represent the two sides of the same coin.

An initial step of venom-induced hypercoagulability with increased thrombin generation could be considered followed by a secondary step of hypocoagulability with B. atrox venom (as shown at H24 with ROTEM data) or persistent hypercoagulability with B. lanceolatus venom (as shown with fibrinography data).

By analogy to the Greek myth of Charybdis and Scylla, Bothrops envenomation may thus follow two opposite clinical expressions: in the event of major platelet and fibrinogen consumption promoting bleeding, hemorrhage will occur early, whereas in case of minor consumption or early restoration of platelet and fibrinogen limiting bleeding, venom-induced systemic inflammation will expose to thrombotic complications of delayed onset.
203.Rationale: The passage directly supports this interpretation. It describes a biphasic process where early coagulopathy (hypercoagulability) leads to either persistent consumption (bleeding) or recovery with inflammation-driven hypercoagulability (thrombosis). This aligns with the observed data on B. atrox (hypocoagulability at H24) and B. lanceolatus (persistent hypercoagulability). The analogy to the Greek myth further reinforces the idea of opposing clinical outcomes based on the balance of these processes.

203.Option: The lack of change in platelet count with B. lanceolatus venom indicates that thrombocytopenia is not a factor in the pathogenesis of thrombosis in this envenomation.
Evidence: A non-significant trend to MCF increase at H24 and a slight hypercoagulable profile were observed with B. lanceolatus venom in our rat model, nevertheless of little contribution to explain the mechanisms of thrombosis in this envenomation.
Based on postmortem data in a B. lanceolatus-bitten patient, direct endothelial damage was hypothesized.
The increase in PAI-1 at H6 and H24 suggests an inhibition of the fibrinolytic pathway but ROTEM analysis may not show fibrinolysis shutdown.
203.Rationale: The passage does not state that thrombocytopenia is absent in B. lanceolatus envenomation; it only notes that platelet count was not significantly altered. However, the pathogenesis of thrombosis in B. lanceolatus envenomation is attributed to factors like inflammation-driven hypercoagulability, fibrinogen dynamics, and PAI-1 increase, not necessarily thrombocytopenia. The option incorrectly assumes that the lack of platelet count change rules out thrombocytopenia as a factor, which is not supported by the text.

203.Option: The recovery of fibrinogen at 24 hours with B. lanceolatus venom is sufficient to explain the onset of thrombosis, regardless of inflammatory or coagulation factors.
Evidence: An increase in plasma fibrinogen at H24 was remarkable in our B. lanceolatus envenomation model.
Thrombosis may be due to coagulation-induced inflammatory activity of the venom.
The increase in PAI-1 at H6 and H24 suggests an inhibition of the fibrinolytic pathway but ROTEM analysis may not show fibrinolysis shutdown.
203.Rationale: The passage indicates that fibrinogen recovery alone is not sufficient to explain thrombosis. It emphasizes the role of inflammation-driven hypercoagulability (e.g., increased IL-6, TNF-α, MCP-1) and fibrinolytic inhibition (PAI-1) in the pathogenesis of thrombosis. The option oversimplifies the mechanism by attributing thrombosis solely to fibrinogen recovery, ignoring the complex interplay of inflammatory and coagulation factors.

203.Option: The similar proteomic composition of B. atrox and B. lanceolatus venoms explains why both species cause early coagulopathy, but differences in venom dose account for the divergent clinical outcomes.
Evidence: Venom composition of these two Bothrops species are relatively similar with comparable activities on coagulation in vitro in the whole blood and plasma.
Our global investigation of hemostasis suggest that mechanisms involved in Bothrops envenomation-induced bleeding and thrombosis are not distinct but represent the two sides of the same coin.
The divergent clinical outcomes (hemorrhage vs. thrombosis) are attributed to species-specific differences and other factors like inflammation and fibrinogen dynamics, not venom dose.
203.Rationale: The passage states that while the venoms have similar proteomic compositions and in vitro coagulant activities, the divergent clinical outcomes are due to differences in the temporal dynamics of hemostasis (e.g., persistent hypocoagulability vs. persistent hypercoagulability) and inflammatory responses, not venom dose. The option incorrectly attributes the outcomes to venom dose differences, which is not supported by the text.

204.Option: The number of microfilariae present in the specific blood spot sample used for DNA extraction
Evidence: Three false negative results were obtained with blood spot samples where mf burden was very low (20–200 mf/mL, or up to 4 organisms in a 20 µL blood spot).
The LLMF72 qPCR assay was able to detect a single mf in a 20 µL dried blood spot (equivalent to a burden of 50 mf/mL).
204.Rationale: While the number of microfilariae is important for detection, the passage emphasizes that the qPCR assay's accuracy is more critically affected by the efficiency of DNA extraction and the volume of blood processed. The study notes that the qPCR assay detected a single mf in a 20 µL blood spot but had false negatives at very low burdens, which were attributed to sampling error or DNA extraction issues rather than the number of mf itself.

204.Option: The efficiency and consistency of DNA extraction methods used on dried blood spots
Evidence: Mf burdens predicted by the LLMF72 qPCR assay were typically 2- to 10-fold lower than microscopic observations, no matter which DNA extraction method was used.
Microfilaremia estimation was most accurate and most consistent with samples subjected to easyMAG extraction.

These predictions were based on a standard curve derived from spiked whole blood samples, further indicating that extraction from dried blood spots is likely less efficient than extraction from fresh whole blood.
204.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the efficiency and consistency of DNA extraction methods significantly impact the accuracy of the qPCR assay. The study found that different extraction methods (e.g., easyMAG) yielded more accurate and consistent results compared to other methods. The lower efficiency of DNA extraction from dried blood spots compared to fresh blood is highlighted as a critical factor affecting assay accuracy.

204.Option: The presence of cross-reactive filarial antigens in the blood sample that interfere with PCR amplification
Evidence: Species specificity of the targets is conferred by a lack of sequence similarity at the primer/probe binding sites.
Our assays are negative with as much as 10 ng of purified genomic DNA (equivalent to 104–105 mf) from B. malayi, W. bancrofti, O. volvulus, or M. perstans.
204.Rationale: The passage indicates that the qPCR assays are highly specific and do not cross-react with other filarial species. The design of the primers and probes ensures species specificity, making cross-reactive antigens an unlikely factor affecting the accuracy of the assay.

204.Option: The timing of blood collection relative to the peak microfilaremia period in the daily cycle
Evidence: Optimum PCR performance requires proper timing of blood collection at mid-day when microfilaremia is greatest.
A microfilaremia kinetics study by Kamgno et al. suggests that smaller numbers of organisms may be detectable outside the window of peak microfilaremia.
204.Rationale: The passage explicitly mentions that the timing of blood collection is critical for optimum PCR performance, as microfilaremia peaks at mid-day. This timing is essential for accurate detection, as lower microfilaremia levels outside the peak period can lead to false negatives. The study also notes that a nomogram may be used to adjust for timing variations, further emphasizing the importance of this factor.

204.Option: The volume of blood processed during DNA extraction from dried blood spots
Evidence: The LLMF72 qPCR assay was able to detect a single mf in a 20 µL dried blood spot (equivalent to a burden of 50 mf/mL).

These predictions were based on a standard curve derived from spiked whole blood samples, further indicating that extraction from dried blood spots is likely less efficient than extraction from fresh whole blood.
It is nonetheless encouraging that boiling blood spots for 10 min enabled detection of as few as 180 mf/mL (7 organisms in a 40 µL blood spot).
204.Rationale: The passage highlights that the volume of blood processed is a critical factor. The study shows that larger volumes (e.g., 100 µL and above) of fresh blood samples yield more accurate results. The lower detection limits with smaller volumes, such as 20 µL or 40 µL blood spots, are noted, indicating that the volume processed directly affects the assay's sensitivity and accuracy.

205.Option: The fact that fructose was detected in 31.6% of male and 30.5% of female Ae. albopictus indicates that general sugar availability in the environment is the dominant factor shaping vector distribution
Evidence: The prevalence of fructose in both sexes was highest in the morning, indicating that morning may be the preferred sugar-feeding time for Ae. albopictus.
Female Ae. albopictus exhibited a lower prevalence of fructose compared to males; a pattern consistent with other mosquito species.
The cold anthrone test detects only recent sugar meals.
Females, due to their larger body size and the energy requirements for reproduction, consumed larger sugar meals when they sugar fed.

The similar prevalence between sexes suggests that sugar availability is sufficient, but does not indicate whether general floral abundance or specific plant communities are more influential. 205.Option: The higher prevalence of flowers and/or fruit on Yorke Island compared to Hammond Island suggests that general floral abundance is the primary driver of Aedes albopictus distribution across the Torres Strait Islands
Evidence: The prevalence of either flowers and/or fruits (χ2 = 46.11, df = 1, p < 0.001) was associated with island, with both being more prevalent on Yorke Island (S3 Fig).
Village habitats had more flowers and/or fruit than bushland habitats (χ2 = 53.99, df = 1, p < 0.001).
205.Rationale: While Yorke Island has higher general floral abundance, the study found that Ae. albopictus abundance was higher on Hammond Island despite lower overall floral abundance. This suggests that specific plant communities, not general floral abundance, are more influential. The significant association between Phyllanthaceae and sugar-feeding behavior further supports this conclusion.

205.Option: The significant association between flower and/or fruit presence and Ae. albopictus abundance in village habitats suggests that general floral availability is the main factor influencing vector distribution
Evidence: The presence of flowers and/or fruit varied significantly across plant families (χ2 = 95.97, df = 13, p < 0.001).

Certain families had notably higher flower and/or fruit prevalence compared with the reference, being: Fabaceae, Asteraceae, Euphorbiaceae, Phyllanthaceae and Poaceae (S4 Fig). This was mostly driven by flower presence.
205.Rationale: The significant association between flower/fruit presence and Ae. albopictus abundance in village habitats is confounded by the fact that certain plant families (like Phyllanthaceae) have higher flower/fruit prevalence. The study's correspondence analysis and PCA results indicate that specific plant families, not general floral abundance, are more strongly associated with Ae. albopictus distribution and sugar-feeding behavior.

205.Option: The strong association between Phyllanthaceae and sugar-feeding behavior, revealed through correspondence analysis, indicates that specific plant community composition drives Aedes albopictus distribution more than general floral abundance
Evidence: The correspondence analysis identified distinct relationships between the proportion of sugar-positive mosquitoes and the presence of flowers by plant family across the study sites.
The first two principal dimensions accounted for a cumulative inertia of 52.5% (28.2% for Dimension 1 and 24.3% for Dimension 2).

209.Option: The bacterium's ability to be genetically modified and produce effector molecules that inhibit Leishmania development
Evidence: Paratransgenesis has been suggested as a feasible strategy for controlling the transmission of pathogens by arthropod vectors. This approach consists of the use of genetically altered symbiotic bacteria that secrete effector molecules that kill the infectious agents.

The analysis presented here showed that dispersal of G. tachinoides across river basins was ∼3 times higher than G. p. gambiensis, which suggests that G. tachinoides flies have the ability to disperse with ease despite the severe fragmentation of the riparian gallery forests in the study area [2].
210.Rationale: While riparian forests are important habitats for tsetse flies, the passage emphasizes that the key factor for re-invasion is the ability of flies to disperse across watershed divides through fragmented savannah habitats, not the presence of riparian forests themselves. The study found that G. tachinoides can disperse across fragmented landscapes more effectively than G. p. gambiensis, which is more sensitive to habitat fragmentation.

210.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that gene flow occurs across watershed divides through fragmented savannah habitats. 210.Rationale: The passage does not indicate that G. tachinoides has a higher degree of local inbreeding compared to G. p. gambiensis. In fact, the genetic data suggest that G. tachinoides has a more panmictic population structure and higher dispersal capacity. The study found that G. tachinoides can disperse more easily across fragmented landscapes, which contradicts the idea of reduced gene flow in this species.

211.Option: Declining seroprevalence in young children signals a growing pool of susceptible individuals, which precedes and predicts the onset of cyclical EV-A71 epidemics
Evidence: We found clear support for our hypothesis, showing that statistically significant decreases in population seroprevalence (as a proxy for immunity) are temporally associated with subsequent epidemics, after adjustment for age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance.

Following a viral epidemic, most of the population at risk would become immune. It may then take 2–3 years for the susceptible population to be replenished by newborns, and to be large enough for the R0 to increase to >1, hence leading to a cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics every 2–3 years.

This is consistent with the significant differences in age-specific EV-A71 seroprevalence seen between non-epidemic and epidemic years in those <2 years old, particularly in the <6 month (from 47.7% to 64.0%, p = 0.016) and 6 months to 1 year age groups (from 35.9% to 64.3%, p = 0.0016).

211.Option: The cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics is primarily driven by antigenic drift in the VP1 protein, as evidenced by the presence of positively selected codons at positions 98 and 145
